sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=12, col=14).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 43 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The click on the red center didn't change the board (board_changed: False). This means clicking on the red dot inside the center cell doesn't do anything. I need to try clicking elsewhere. 
Looking at the board structure:
- Four 3x3 grids
- The bottom-right grid is inside a charcoal frame - this might be the "target" or "output" area
- The center cells have special patterns (gray-bordered white with red dot)
- The bottom-right grid is all blue cells
Maybe I need to click on the regular grid cells (the colored 6x6 squares) rather than the center markers. Or maybe I need to click on the charcoal frame area. Or perhaps the gray corner brackets.
Let me try clicking on one of the regular grid cells first - like a blue cell in the top-left grid. The blue cell at position (2,4) is at rows 2-7, cols 4-9. Its center would be around row 4-5, col 5-6.
Actually, let me reconsider the structure. The ~43 target actions suggests this is a fairly complex level. Let me think about what 43 actions could mean...
Maybe I need to click on each cell to change its color, or click on specific cells to match a pattern. The bottom-right grid being all blue and framed might be the "solution template" or the area to work on.
Let me try clicking on a regular cell in the bottom-right grid (the framed one) since that seems to be the special area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Clicking the red center did nothing. Let me try clicking on regular grid cells.
# The bottom-right grid (framed) has all blue cells - let me check their positions
# and try clicking one there.

# First let me find all 36px cells in the bottom-right area
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
br_cells = []
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 36:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_start = b[0][0]
        c_start = b[0][1]
        # Bottom-right area: rows 36+, cols 38+
        if r_start >= 36 and c_start >= 38:
            br_cells.append((n['id'], n['color'], r_start, c_start))

print("Bottom-right 36px cells:")
for c in br_cells:
    print(f"  id={c[0]}, color={c[1]}, start=({c[2]},{c[3]})")

# Also check the charcoal frame node (node 34) and gray corner brackets
print("\nCharcoal frame (node 34) boundary:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] == 34:
        print(f"  {n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'g' and n['pixels'] == 20:
        print(f"  Gray bracket node {n['id']}: {n['boundary']}")

# The bottom-right grid cells are all blue (b)
# Let me try clicking on the center of a blue cell in the bottom-right grid
# First blue cell: starts at (36, 38), center ~ (39, 41)
# But wait - the center cell of bottom-right grid has a special marker too
# Let me check what's at rows 44-49, cols 46-51 (center of bottom-right grid)

# Let me try clicking on a regular blue cell in the bottom-right grid
# Cell at (36, 38) - top-left of the 3x3 grid
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 39, 'col': 41}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE_red_center": "No effect - clicking red center of center cell does nothing"}, "object_taxonomy": {"grid_cell": "6x6 colored square (36px)", "center_marker": "2x2 red square inside gray-bordered white cell", "charcoal_frame": "Dark gray border around bottom-right grid", "gray_bracket": "Gray L-shaped corner brackets"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "Orange bar at row 63"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Four 3x3 grids. Bottom-right grid is framed and all blue. Center cells have gray-bordered white with red 2x2. Clicking red center had no effect.", "goal_model": "Need to discover what clicking does - try regular cells", "recent_findings": ["Clicking red center (12,14) had no effect"], "open_questions": ["What cells are clickable?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking a blue cell in bottom-right framed grid", "strategy_log": ["Clicked red center - no effect"], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on red center (12,14) - no board change"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
